title: Helsinki Event Aggregator
tagline: A faster way to pull together event data from across the city.
problem: Helsinki's event data is spread across many different sources. Checking them one by one meant the app took over a minute to load.
solution: I used Python to fetch data from every source at the same time. This dropped the wait time from 60 seconds to less than five.
architecture: An AWS Lambda function gathers and organizes the data as soon as it is requested. I also set up a workflow to handle updates and deployment automatically.

title: Secure File Submission API
tagline: A secure way to handle file uploads without risking malware.
problem: Public forms that let people upload files are a big security risk. Without a check, someone could easily upload a virus to the server.
solution: I built a backend that automatically sends every uploaded file to be scanned for viruses. It only sends the file to the admin if it comes back clean.
architecture: I used FastAPI to handle the uploads and VirusTotal to scan them in the background. It checks the file type and safety before sending it off via email. I also put everything in a Docker container for easy setup.

title: IMDb CLI Manager
tagline: A command-line tool for managing a personal movie and review database.
problem: I needed to understand how NoSQL databases work, and managing a movie collection can get messy, especially when you need to keep reviews and movie details in sync while using a cloud database.
solution: I created a Python tool that connects to a cloud database. It handles adding movies, calculating average ratings, and making sure reviews are deleted if a movie is removed.
architecture: The app connects to MongoDB Atlas using PyMongo. I built helper functions to keep the code clean and added logic to make sure the database stays organized when data is deleted.

title: Dynamic Social Network
tagline: A social platform where users can post, follow, and interact in real-time.
problem: I needed to learn how modern web development works because traditional web apps often feel slow because the whole page has to reload every time you like a post or add a comment.
solution: I used JavaScript to handle likes and comments in the background. This makes the app feel fast because users don't have to wait for page reloads.
architecture: The project combines a Django backend with JavaScript to update the feed without reloading. I also used Bootstrap to make sure it looks good on phones and computers.
